news_summary: Today's human rights news highlights inspiring efforts to challenge segregation through inclusive housing initiatives and the impactful journey of a former refugee advocating for displaced people worldwide.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité des droits de l'homme d'aujourd'hui met en lumière des initiatives inspirantes visant à lutter contre la ségrégation grâce à des projets de logement inclusifs, ainsi que le parcours remarquable d'un ancien réfugié qui défend la cause des personnes déplacées à travers le monde.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan iniciativas inspiradoras para combatir la segregación mediante proyectos de vivienda inclusiva, así como la impactante trayectoria de un antiguo refugiado que aboga por las personas desplazadas en todo el mundo.

articles.title: The UN housing development which challenged 1940s’ segregated United States
articles.summary: At a time when some state laws dictated where different races could live, Parkway Village, built to house some of the first UN staff in New York in 1947, led the way in eliminating racially segregated housing in the United States.
articles.content:
In the United States of that period, laws in many states enforced separate schools, transportation and bathrooms based solely on race. The military was still segregated, laws prohibiting interracial marriage remained in place and many housing developments enforced “whites-only” policies.

One early resident and later UN staff member, Carlos Figueroa, remembered being friends with children from Europe, the Middle East, Asia, Africa, Latin Ameria and the Caribbean. Growing up together, they sampled cuisines, learned about their cultures and picked up on small bits of their languages.

By 1952, nearly 500 United Nations families resided in Parkway.

“It was enlightening to see kids from countries and cultures which are traditional rivals - Indians and Pakistanis, Arabs and Jews, for example – playing together, attending the same schools and, if not learning to love and trust one another, at least finding a way to get along in an atmosphere of cooperation and understanding,” Mr. Figueroa said.

UN News/Daniel Dickinson Parkway Village in New York was the city's first racially integrated housing estate.

Among the Parkway’s quaint low-rise homes, winding paths and open lawns lived staffers from over 50 countries, including Nobel Laureate Ralph Bunche.

“Since its earliest days, the United Nations has sought to be a leader in eliminating racial discrimination worldwide,” said Rula Hinedi, head of the UN tour guides, who recently led a fact-finding mission to Parkway Village.

“There are few clearer intentions to put this principle into practice than the development of Parkway Village when the UN first decided to permanently locate to New York in December 1946.”

Confronting segregation in NYC

In the immediate aftermath of the Second World War, New York City faced a serious housing crisis, and this was compounded by the return of 900,000 US servicemen from overseas.

“New York seemed to be 150,000 to 250,000 apartment units short of the housing needs that were required at the time and yet there were thousands of diplomats getting ready to make their home in New York City,” New York historian, Chris McNickle, told UN News.

However, it was the requirement from the United Nations for the host city to provide housing for staff without discrimination that would cause an even greater challenge. Many housing developments in New York, such as Manhattan’s famous Stuy-Town or Fresh Meadows, practised racial segregation.

The organization knew that it would need somewhere to accommodate its incredibly diverse staff, especially at a time when “it was very difficult for black people to secure an apartment, sometimes impossible,” Mr. McNickle said.

Parkway Village, then just a plot of land nestled deep in a quiet corner of the borough of Queens, was the solution that the UN and the City of New York came up with.

UN Archives An historical map depicts Parkway Village, the UN's racially integrated housing development.

The United Nations Village

Built from scratch in 1947 on 34 acres of undeveloped land, Parkway comprised 687 apartments sparsely located in small groups across the property, with views in every direction.

Described by current resident, Judith Guttman, as the “country in the city”, the Village fostered a “communal” atmosphere for its residents. With buildings covering just 15 per cent of the space and the UN school and nursery on site until the early 1980s, Ms. Guttman said “it was so community-oriented and had a very culturally open atmosphere, that for many years no fences between the homes were built...it was the perfect place to raise kids.”

‘Atmosphere of cooperation and understanding’

Beyond the familial atmosphere, the development also provided a haven for civil rights activists, Nobel laureates and UN staff from countless countries who wouldn't usually have been able to live together given the racial laws in place in that period.

One UN staff member who benefited from the village’s integration was Ralph Bunche, the first Black person to win the Nobel Peace Prize following his mediation efforts on behalf of the UN in the late 1940s Arab-Israeli conflict.

Moving past discrimination

As Parkway Village continues as an historical symbol of the UN’s push to promote racial equality, the UN independent human rights expert on contemporary forms of racism, Dr. Ashwini K.P., said that progress has been made.

“Over the past 80 years, the world has moved from openly codified racism toward a global consensus that racial discrimination is unacceptable,” she said.

However, speaking ahead of the International Day for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination, marked annually on 21 March, she added “racism continues to manifest in different forms” and “shape access to education, healthcare, economic opportunity, and political power.”

“Ending racial discrimination requires sustained political will, measurable accountability and a commitment to equality that is lived and not merely declared,” she said. “Confronting it openly is how we diminish its power and reclaim our collective humanity.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a historically significant housing development, Parkway Village, which challenged and helped dismantle racial segregation in the United States in the 1940s. It reports a real-world, meaningful impact on racial equality and integration, with broad societal significance and detailed context about the development's role in fostering racial harmony and inclusion. The story is focused, substantive, and inspiring in its demonstration of progress against systemic racism.
articles.personality_title: Parkway Village: A 1940s housing project that broke US racial barriers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the 1940s, many parts of the United States had laws that kept people of different races apart. This included where they could live, go to school, and use public facilities. Housing was often segregated, with many places only allowing white people to live there.

**What happened** – In 1947, the United Nations helped create Parkway Village in Queens, New York, to house its diverse staff. This housing development welcomed people from more than 50 countries and was one of the first racially integrated neighborhoods in the city. Families of different races lived together, children played and went to school side by side, and the community shared cultures and languages.

**Impact** – Parkway Village was unique because it challenged the widespread segregation of the time. It showed that people from different backgrounds could live peacefully and cooperatively. This included notable residents like Ralph Bunche, the first Black Nobel Peace Prize winner. The village became a symbol of hope and progress during a period when racial discrimination was common.

**What's next step** – Parkway Village remains an important reminder of the fight against racial segregation. Experts say that while laws have changed, racism still exists in new forms today. Continued efforts are needed to ensure equality in education, healthcare, jobs, and politics. The story of Parkway Village encourages ongoing work toward fair treatment for all.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Parkway Village was a groundbreaking housing community in the 1940s that helped break racial segregation in the United States by bringing diverse families together to live and grow as neighbors.
articles.personality_title_fr: Parkway Village : un projet de logement des années 1940 qui a brisé les barrières raciales aux États-Unis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans les années 1940, de nombreuses régions des États-Unis avaient des lois qui séparaient les personnes selon leur race, notamment où elles pouvaient vivre, aller à l'école et utiliser les lieux publics. Les logements étaient souvent ségrégués, beaucoup étant réservés uniquement aux Blancs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 1947, l’Organisation des Nations Unies a aidé à créer Parkway Village dans le Queens, à New York, pour loger son personnel diversifié. Ce quartier accueillait des habitants de plus de 50 pays et fut l’un des premiers quartiers intégrés racialement de la ville. Des familles de différentes origines vivaient ensemble, les enfants jouaient et allaient à l’école côte à côte, partageant cultures et langues.

**Impact** – Parkway Village était unique car il a défié la ségrégation très répandue à cette époque. Il a montré que des personnes de différents horizons pouvaient vivre en paix et en coopération. Parmi ses habitants figurait Ralph Bunche, premier Noir lauréat du prix Nobel de la paix. Le village est devenu un symbole d’espoir et de progrès durant une période marquée par la discrimination raciale.

**Prochaine étape** – Parkway Village reste un rappel important de la lutte contre la ségrégation raciale. Des experts soulignent que, même si les lois ont changé, le racisme existe encore sous de nouvelles formes. Il faut continuer à lutter pour l’égalité dans l’éducation, la santé, l’emploi et la politique. L’histoire de Parkway Village encourage à poursuivre ce combat.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Parkway Village fut une communauté résidentielle novatrice dans les années 1940 qui a aidé à briser la ségrégation raciale aux États-Unis en réunissant des familles diverses pour vivre et grandir ensemble.
articles.personality_title_es: Parkway Village: un proyecto de viviendas de los años 40 que rompió barreras raciales en EE.UU.
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la década de 1940, muchas partes de Estados Unidos tenían leyes que separaban a las personas según su raza, incluyendo dónde podían vivir, estudiar y usar lugares públicos. Las viviendas a menudo estaban segregadas, y muchos sitios solo permitían vivir a personas blancas.

**Qué pasó** – En 1947, las Naciones Unidas ayudaron a crear Parkway Village en Queens, Nueva York, para alojar a su personal diverso. Este vecindario recibió a personas de más de 50 países y fue uno de los primeros barrios racialmente integrados de la ciudad. Familias de diferentes razas vivían juntas, los niños jugaban y estudiaban juntos, compartiendo culturas e idiomas.

**Impacto** – Parkway Village fue único porque desafió la segregación generalizada de la época. Demostró que personas de diferentes orígenes podían vivir en paz y cooperación. Entre sus residentes estuvo Ralph Bunche, el primer afroamericano en ganar el Premio Nobel de la Paz. El barrio se convirtió en un símbolo de esperanza y progreso durante un tiempo marcado por la discriminación racial.

**Próximo paso** – Parkway Village sigue siendo un recordatorio importante de la lucha contra la segregación racial. Expertos señalan que, aunque las leyes cambiaron, el racismo sigue presente en nuevas formas. Es necesario continuar trabajando para lograr igualdad en educación, salud, empleo y política. La historia de Parkway Village anima a seguir adelante con esta tarea.

**Frase clave** – Parkway Village fue una comunidad de viviendas innovadora en los años 40 que ayudó a romper la segregación racial en Estados Unidos al reunir a familias diversas para vivir y crecer juntas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a diverse cluster of quaint mid-20th-century low-rise homes with open lawns and winding paths, each home subtly marked by distinct cultural patterns or architectural details reflecting over 50 countries, connected by intertwining colorful threads symbolizing unity and cooperation, set against a calm Queens neighborhood backdrop under soft natural tones.

articles.title: A former refugee takes on a world in flight
articles.summary: Barham Salih has spent much of his life crossing borders, first as a young Kurdish exile fleeing repression in Iraq, and now as the UN top official for refugees, confronting a world in which more than 117 million people have been forced from their homes.
articles.content:
He wasted no time getting out into the field. Within days of taking office on 1 January, he had already left the conference rooms of his Geneva headquarters for the dust of refugee camps in Kenya and Chad, a signal of how he intends to lead an agency stretched by crises that are multiplying faster than the system built to respond to them.

“The responsibility in every sense of the word is awesome,” he said in a recent interview, his voice catching slightly on the enormity of the task.

For Mr. Salih, in his mid-sixties, the role is anything but abstract. The new High Commissioner for Refugees knows displacement not as a statistic but as a lived experience.

‘Behind every statistic is a life’

Born in Iraqi Kurdistan in 1960, he became a refugee himself as a teenager and spent years in exile, part of a generation shaped by repression and war under Saddam Hussein’s rule. He studied in the United Kingdom, built a political career and eventually returned home, rising to become Iraq’s eighth president in 2018, a trajectory that now informs how he sees the millions still trapped in limbo.

“Behind every statistic is a life,” he said, “a person with aspiration, with a right to dignity, with the right to a better future.”

That insistence on individual dignity, like a refrain, runs through his first months on the job. But, so is a harder truth: the global system built to respond to displacement is under strain. Even as displacement rises, humanitarian funding is tightening, forcing agencies like his to stretch already limited resources across expanding needs.

A crisis that no longer ends

For decades, the architecture of refugee protection rested on the assumption that displacement was a stopgap. People fled, received protection and eventually returned home when it was safe to do so.

“Being a refugee is not meant to be a fate,” Mr. Salih said. “It is meant to be a temporary condition.”

But, as conflicts drag on and political settlements stall, that premise has quietly collapsed. Today, nearly two thirds of refugees live in what humanitarian agencies call “protracted displacement”: five, 10, even 20 years or more without a durable solution. Entire childhoods unfold in camps. Generations grow up without ever seeing the homes their families fled.

The UN refugee chief does not soften the diagnosis.

“That is not an acceptable situation,” he said. “This is a violation of the basic human rights to dignity.”

© UNHCR Video UN High Commissioner for Refugees, Barham Salih, on a visit to refugees in the Zaatari camp in Jordan.

Ambitious plan

His plan is ambitious. He has set a goal of reducing by half, within a decade, the number of people in long-term displacement dependent on humanitarian assistance, a target that far exceeds the ability or resources of his agency alone.

“I know, and I understand full well, that this is far beyond the means and the capabilities of [UNHCR] today,” he acknowledged.

The strategy hinges on something the humanitarian system has long struggled to achieve: moving beyond emergency aid toward economic inclusion. Refugees, he argues, must be able to work and contribute to the societies that host them rather than remain dependent on assistance.

This would require a broad coalition, including development banks, private investors, donor governments and host countries, many of which are themselves under economic strain. It would also require a shift in political will at a time when many wealthier nations are tightening borders rather than expanding opportunity.

The weight of hosting

One of the enduring paradoxes of the refugee crisis is that it is largely borne by countries least equipped to handle it.

“We need to help host nations who are mostly, by the way, low-income, middle-income countries,” Mr. Salih said.

From Colombia to Uganda, from Chad to Bangladesh, these countries absorb the vast majority of displaced people, often with insufficient international support. Their schools, hospitals and labour markets stretch to accommodate newcomers even as their own citizens face economic hardship.

The UN refugee chief speaks of these host communities with a mix of admiration and urgency.

“I am humbled by the generosity of many of these host nations and communities,” he said.

But, generosity can only go so far. Without sustained investment and inclusion, the system risks hardening into permanent crisis, with a global underclass of the displaced being warehoused rather than welcomed.

© UNHCR/Hélène Caux The UN Refugees chief, Barham Salih (centre), speaks with Sudanese refugees at a women's centre in Farchana, Chad.

A message to the displaced and the world

In Kakuma, a refugee camp in northern Kenya, one of the world’s largest and home to around 300,000 people, and across Turkish cities hosting Syrians more than a decade after their exodus, Mr. Salih says he has seen something that resists the language of despair.

“The story of resilience with every refugee I have met is genuine and is real,” he said.

It is this resilience that shapes his message, particularly to young refugees growing up in uncertainty.

“To the young people, I say we are going to be working to help you with your agency,” he said, emphasising not just protection but possibility.

The word “agency” is deliberate. It signals a departure from seeing refugees solely as victims, toward recognising them as actors in their own futures. But, it also places a responsibility on the international community to create the conditions in which that agency can be exercised.

“A refugee is meant to be a temporary situation, not a permanent pain.”

For now, those conditions remain uneven at best.

Conflicts continue to erupt, including the latest escalation in the Middle East. Humanitarian budgets are shrinking. Political consensus is fraying and the number of displaced people keeps climbing, each figure representing, as Mr. Salih insists, a life interrupted.

At the end of his early travels, what stayed with him was not only the scale of the crisis, but its persistence.

“Once again,” he said, returning to the idea that frames his mission, “a refugee is meant to be a temporary situation, not a permanent pain.”

For millions of people living in camps like Kakuma, that distinction has already blurred.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the appointment and active leadership of Barham Salih, a former refugee and current UN High Commissioner for Refugees, who is addressing the global refugee crisis with an ambitious plan to reduce long-term displacement and promote economic inclusion. This represents a significant, real-world effort to improve the dignity and futures of millions of displaced people worldwide, emphasizing resilience and systemic change. The story focuses on a meaningful humanitarian challenge with broad societal impact and provides detailed context on the issue and proposed solutions.
articles.personality_title: Former refugee leads UN effort to change lives of displaced people
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Millions of people around the world are forced to leave their homes because of war, violence, or danger. Many live in refugee camps for years, often without a clear way back to their homes. The United Nations helps these people through an agency called UNHCR, which supports refugees worldwide.

**What happened** – Barham Salih, who was once a refugee himself, became the head of UNHCR on January 1. Soon after, he visited refugee camps in Kenya and Chad to see the situation firsthand. Salih grew up fleeing danger in Iraq and later became the president of Iraq. Now, he uses his experience to lead efforts to help millions of displaced people.

**Impact** – Salih wants to cut in half the number of refugees who stay in camps or rely on aid for many years. His plan is to help refugees work and live better in the countries that host them, instead of only receiving emergency help. This is important because many refugees now stay displaced for 5, 10, or even 20 years, losing chances to build a normal life. The countries hosting refugees often have few resources themselves, so Salih calls for more support and cooperation from the world.

**What's next step** – To reach his goal, Salih needs help from governments, banks, and businesses to create jobs and chances for refugees. He also wants to change how people think about refugees, seeing them as active people who can contribute to society. This will take time and effort, especially as funding is tight and conflicts continue.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Barham Salih, a former refugee leading the UN refugee agency, aims to reduce long-term displacement by promoting work and inclusion for refugees worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un ancien réfugié dirige les efforts de l’ONU pour changer la vie des déplacés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des millions de personnes dans le monde sont forcées de quitter leur domicile à cause de la guerre, de la violence ou du danger. Beaucoup vivent des années dans des camps de réfugiés, souvent sans perspective de retour. L’ONU aide ces personnes grâce à l’agence appelée HCR, qui soutient les réfugiés partout dans le monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Barham Salih, lui-même ancien réfugié, est devenu chef du HCR le 1er janvier. Peu après, il a visité des camps de réfugiés au Kenya et au Tchad pour voir la situation de près. Salih a grandi en fuyant le danger en Irak et est devenu plus tard président de l’Irak. Aujourd’hui, il utilise son expérience pour diriger les efforts en faveur des millions de déplacés.

**Impact** – Salih veut réduire de moitié le nombre de réfugiés qui restent longtemps dans les camps ou dépendent de l’aide. Son plan est d’aider les réfugiés à travailler et à mieux vivre dans les pays qui les accueillent, plutôt que de recevoir seulement une aide d’urgence. C’est important car beaucoup de réfugiés restent déplacés pendant 5, 10 ou même 20 ans, perdant la chance de mener une vie normale. Les pays qui accueillent les réfugiés ont souvent peu de ressources, donc Salih appelle à plus de soutien et de coopération mondiale.

**Prochaine étape** – Pour atteindre son objectif, Salih a besoin de l’aide des gouvernements, des banques et des entreprises pour créer des emplois et des opportunités pour les réfugiés. Il veut aussi changer la façon dont on voit les réfugiés, en les considérant comme des personnes actives qui peuvent contribuer à la société. Cela prendra du temps et des efforts, surtout avec un financement limité et des conflits qui continuent.

**En une phrase** – Barham Salih, ancien réfugié à la tête de l’agence de l’ONU, veut réduire le déplacement de longue durée en favorisant le travail et l’inclusion des réfugiés dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Un ex refugiado lidera el esfuerzo de la ONU para cambiar vidas de desplazados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Millones de personas en el mundo se ven obligadas a dejar sus hogares por guerras, violencia o peligro. Muchas viven años en campos de refugiados, a menudo sin una forma clara de regresar a casa. Las Naciones Unidas ayudan a estas personas a través de una agencia llamada ACNUR, que apoya a los refugiados en todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – Barham Salih, quien fue refugiado en su juventud, se convirtió en jefe de ACNUR el 1 de enero. Poco después, visitó campos de refugiados en Kenia y Chad para conocer la situación de cerca. Salih creció huyendo del peligro en Irak y luego fue presidente de Irak. Ahora usa su experiencia para liderar los esfuerzos para ayudar a millones de desplazados.

**Impacto** – Salih quiere reducir a la mitad el número de refugiados que permanecen en campos o dependen de ayuda durante muchos años. Su plan es ayudar a los refugiados a trabajar y vivir mejor en los países que los acogen, en lugar de solo recibir ayuda de emergencia. Esto es importante porque muchos refugiados llevan 5, 10 o incluso 20 años desplazados, perdiendo oportunidades para una vida normal. Los países que los acogen suelen tener pocos recursos, por eso Salih pide más apoyo y cooperación global.

**Próximo paso** – Para lograr su objetivo, Salih necesita ayuda de gobiernos, bancos y empresas para crear empleos y oportunidades para los refugiados. También quiere cambiar la forma en que se ve a los refugiados, reconociéndolos como personas activas que pueden contribuir a la sociedad. Esto tomará tiempo y esfuerzo, especialmente con fondos limitados y conflictos que continúan.

**Una frase para recordar** – Barham Salih, un ex refugiado que lidera la agencia de la ONU, busca reducir el desplazamiento prolongado promoviendo el trabajo y la inclusión de refugiados en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vast, gently sunlit refugee camp with humble tents and scattered belongings, where a large, sturdy tree with deep roots symbolizes resilience and hope, its branches stretching toward a distant horizon representing a brighter, inclusive future beyond displacement.
